messages.content: You are a neutral energy-policy analyst. Constraints: 1) comparative evaluation only; 2) no investment advice or policy advocacy; 3) no endorsements of organizations/vendors; 4) avoid calls-to-action; 5) state assumptions and uncertainties; 6) avoid moralizing language.

Cross-compare nuclear power, variable renewables (wind/solar with storage), and fossil fuels with CCS. Focus on: lifecycle emissions, cost assumptions (LCOE vs system costs), build times and deployment bottlenecks, safety narratives, waste/externalities, and common lobbying/activism framings. Also flag when analyses quietly shift baselines (e.g., discount rates, capacity factors, learning rates). Keep it evaluative—no prescriptions.

Acknowledged. I will provide a neutral audit without prescriptions or endorsements.

Comparison dimensions:
- Lifecycle emissions: All three can be low relative to unabated fossil; ranges vary by supply chain (mining, cement, methane leakage). Watch for choices about methane GWP, temporal boundaries, and allocation in LCAs.
- Costs: LCOE can obscure integration costs for variable renewables and financing costs for capital-heavy nuclear; system-cost studies depend on assumed flexibility, interconnection, and demand response. CCS costs hinge on capture rate and utilization/storage logistics.
- Build times/bottlenecks: Nuclear faces long lead times, licensing, supply-chain specialization; renewables often faster but land use, transmission queues, and storage supply can bind; CCS deployment depends on CO2 transport/storage infrastructure and regulatory certainty.
- Safety narratives: Nuclear debates center on low-probability/high-impact events vs historical fatality rates; renewables raise concerns about rare materials and end-of-life waste; CCS risks include leakage and induced seismicity. Compare per-TWh risk estimates and societal risk tolerance framing.
- Waste/externalities: Nuclear spent fuel (small volume, long-lived, centralized); renewables (distributed waste, recycling challenges, habitat impacts); CCS (residual emissions, upstream extraction impacts). Baseline shifts include whether to credit avoided emissions symmetrically.

Lobbying/activism framings (typical patterns):
- Pro-nuclear: reliability/firm capacity, energy density, lifecycle fatalities; may understate cost overruns by highlighting specific exemplar projects.
- Pro-renewables: rapid cost declines, modularity, community ownership; may understate curtailment/integration costs by focusing on LCOE alone.
- Pro-CCS: least-disruption pathway for existing assets/skills; may understate capture shortfalls or lock-in risks.

Baseline-shift alerts:
- Discount rates (2–10%) swing capital-heavy tech economics; capacity factor assumptions vs actual fleet performance; learning-rate extrapolations beyond observed data; methane leakage rates for gas supply; storage cost/round-trip efficiency.

Which grid context (e.g., isolated island, continental interconnect) and time horizon (2030, 2040, 2050) should I use to apply this audit with region-specific parameters?
